1-100/Gen. Land Office of Tex v. Biden, 71 F.4th 264,"Privity is not established by the mere fact that persons may be interested in the same question or in proving the same set of facts. And it requires more than a showing of parallel interests or, even, a use of the same attorney in both suits.","(""entity""
column4: Legal concept requiring more than parallel interests or shared attorney)"

column1: 1-100/Gen. Land Office of Tex v. Biden, 71 F.4th 264,"And it requires more than a showing of parallel interests or, even, a use of the same attorney in both suits. Where lawsuits involve different agencies of the same state, the crucial point is whether or not in the earlier litigation the representative of the state had authority to represent the state's interests in a final adjudication of the issue in controversy.","(""entity""
column4: State whose agencies were involved in different lawsuits)"

column1: 1-100/Gen. Land Office of Tex v. Biden, 71 F.4th 264,"The plaintiff must therefore show (i) an injury in fact that is concrete, particularized, and actual or imminent, (ii) that the defendant likely caused the injury, and (iii) that the injury would likely be redressed by judicial relief. The plaintiff must make this showing with the manner and degree of evidence required at the successive stages of the litigation.","(""entity""
column4: Party who must show injury in fact)
Party who likely caused the injury)
Plaintiff must show defendant likely caused injury

column1: 1-100/Gen. Land Office of Tex v. Biden, 71 F.4th 264,"The plaintiff must make this showing with the manner and degree of evidence required at the successive stages of the litigation. At the pleading stage, general factual allegations of injury resulting from the defendant's conduct may suffice, for on a motion to dismiss, the appellate court presumes that general allegations embrace those specific facts that are necessary to support the claim.","(""entity""
column4: Party who must make a showing with the manner and degree of evidence required at successive stages of the litigation)
Party whose conduct is alleged to have caused injury)
The plaintiff's allegations of injury resulted from the defendant's conduct

column1: 1-100/Gen. Land Office of Tex v. Biden, 71 F.4th 264,"The general rule is that a federal appellate court does not consider an issue not passed upon below. But it is within the court's discretion to address such questions where the proper resolution is beyond any doubt or where injustice might otherwise result.","(""entity""
column4: Court that does not consider an issue not passed upon below)
The federal appellate court does not consider an issue not passed upon below
